title: The Seven Sages of Rome (Midland Version)
summary: The checked-in CME00018 XML represents the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item keyed from Thomas Wright's edition The Seven Sages, in English Verse. Wright's poem text appeared as Percy Society publication no. 53 in January 1845; a separately issued introduction and notes followed in 1846 and were bound with it. Wright transcribed the anonymous Middle English Midland Version from Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17, now vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb (legacy manuscript pp. 594–612). The witness is a shared parchment miscellany, not a work-exclusive codex. A complete public scan of the bound printed object and a complete scan of Cambridge's 1856 catalogue volume are cached locally with exact work portions. No publicly obtainable complete manuscript facsimile was verified on 2026-07-11; onsite consultation and mediated Cambridge imaging remain the documented routes.
review_notes: The intellectual subject is the anonymous Middle English Midland Version, Text D, not the translingual Seven Sages tradition as a whole, Wright's editorial apparatus, the complete Dd.1.17 miscellany, or either digital encoding.
Current foliation and legacy continuous pagination are both retained. Their exact page-by-page crosswalk has not been visually verified because no complete public manuscript facsimile was found.
The XML preserves Wright's printed line-number anomaly at p. 52, where 1500 is misprinted as 500; this is not silently corrected into a monotonic milestone sequence.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00018
notes: The file is 143,987 bytes and is pinned through the CME submodule commit recorded above.
Its TEMPHEAD records a 2016-06-21 review, not the original creation date of the electronic transcription.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text CME00018

date_statements.label: documented XML review
date_statements.notes: The TEMPHEAD revision entry documents review and correction work; it does not establish the initial keying date.

notes: The encoding includes Wright's 1845 title page and poem on printed pp. 1–116, but excludes the separately issued introduction, printed notes on pp. 117–118, and Wright's preliminary editor notice.
It contains 3,455 L elements in 16 LG elements, 15 DIV2 elements typed tale, 117 PB elements, and 342 printed line-number MILESTONE elements.

entities.label: Thomas Wright's 1845–1846 edition of The Seven Sages
alternate_labels: The Seven Sages, in English Verse

date_statements.label: separately issued introduction and completed bound publication

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, ed., The Seven Sages, in English Verse, edited from a manuscript in the Public Library of the University of Cambridge (London: printed for the Percy Society by T. Richards, 1845–1846), Percy Society Publications nos. 53 and 64; Early English Poetry, vol. 16.
bibliographic.title: The Seven Sages, in English Verse
bibliographic.series: Percy Society Publications; Early English Poetry, vol. 16
bibliographic.publisher: Printed for the Percy Society by T. Richards

notes: The 1845 issue title identifies Percy Society publication no. 53 and January 1845. Folger records that the introduction was issued separately in 1846 as no. 64.
Wright states that he corrected some scribal errors sparingly, restored a detached n to the preceding article, and separated words that the manuscript joined.
Printed notes on pp. 117–118 document further readings, supplied letters, defective lines, and emendations; those notes are not part of the U-M encoding.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17

date_statements.label: production
date_statements.value: second half of the fourteenth century (1350–1400)
date_statements.notes: The 1856 catalogue assigns the hand to the second half or close of the fourteenth century; the current scholarly database normalizes the production range to 1350–1400.

holding.notes: The Seven Sages component is now in vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb. The 1856 catalogue locates the same component at legacy manuscript pp. 594–612. No current repository-owned exact online component record was located; Cambridge's official search guidance directs most medieval-manuscript research to the printed catalogue.

physical_description.extent: Current description: vol. 1, 261 folios; vol. 2, 93 folios; vol. 3, 87 folios. Legacy 1856 description: 424 leaves, paginated 1–848.
physical_description.format: large folio manuscript, now divided into three volumes
notes: The Seven Sages occupies vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb, nineteen manuscript pages in three columns of about sixty lines each.
The 1856 catalogue calls this portion carelessly transcribed in a running hand of the second half of the fourteenth century.
The modern database records no illustrations and twenty-four other texts in the three-volume miscellany, including Piers Plowman and Mandeville's Travels immediately before this work in vol. 3.
This is the sole surviving witness of the Midland Version identified by current scholarship.

The witness date, England production, Midland language association, physical extent, and image terms remain witness-layer facts and are not transferred wholesale to the abstract Seven Sages tradition.

locators: Printed catalogue pp. 15–26 (PDF pages 35–46; scan leaves 37–48)
Seven Sages component description at printed pp. 25–26 (PDF pages 45–46; scan leaves 47–48)

access.access_method: consultation in the Special Collections Reading Room by registered Library users
Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory imaging and permissions request
access.format: uncompressed JPEG at 600 ppi, 1:1 RGB, or TIFF on request, subject to condition and quotation
notes: Request complete physical-object coverage—binding and spine where offered, boards or covers, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf in all three current volumes, and adjacent works—then map CME00018 separately to vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb.
Image supply does not itself confer publication or redistribution permission; Cambridge requires a separate use-permission choice for uses beyond private study.

access.access_method: dated search of Cambridge Digital Library, Cambridge collection guidance, exact shelfmark searches, and the current Seven Sages database record
notes: No manuscript image was cached. This negative finding is search-bounded and does not prove that Cambridge lacks internal photography or an unindexed surrogate.
A Cambridge exhibition exposes one image of Dd.1.17, f. 129r from a different work and volume context; it is not a facsimile of the CME00018 leaves and was not substituted for the whole object.

access.access_method: controlled digital lending / preview; unrestricted source files are private

notes: No Whitelock file was cached because the public item exposes preview/controlled-lending access rather than an unrestricted downloadable source PDF.

rights.basis: The item page states that the materials are in the public domain and links the DPLA No Copyright–United States rights statement.
notes: This records the provider's U.S.-specific assertion; it does not independently dedicate all XML markup, interface assets, or third-party reproductions worldwide.

rights.component: 1845–1846 printed text, introduction, notes, and typographical arrangement
rights.basis: The London edition appeared in 1845–1846. U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A describes a maximum 95-year term for works already under statutory protection before 1978 and notes the separate restoration rules for some foreign works; that maximum term has elapsed. U-M independently marks this item No Copyright–United States.

rights.component: Thomas Wright's editorial text and the edition's typographical arrangement
rights.basis: Thomas Wright died in 1877; current UK guidance gives written works a usual term of life plus 70 years and published typographical arrangements 25 years from publication.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and interface assets
rights.basis: The exact PDF is publicly downloadable, but the inspected item metadata provides no item-level open license for the digital scan as such.
notes: Public download and local research caching do not by themselves establish redistribution permission for the scan file.

rights.basis: Cambridge distinguishes private-study supply from permission for academic, commercial, publication, broadcast, and other use, and supplies images only after quotation and payment.

rights.component: 2005 critical edition, introduction, commentary, notes, and typographical arrangement
rights.basis: The edition was newly published in 2005 and no open license was found; Internet Archive exposes controlled lending rather than unrestricted source files.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Wright's combined publication includes an introduction, editor notice, poem, and notes within a larger bound scan object.
editorial_practices.representation: The XML retains the 1845 title page and poem only.
editorial_practices.description: The electronic encoding omits Wright's separately issued introduction, preliminary editor notice, printed notes, and all adjacent bound publications while preserving the complete poem text on pp. 1–116.

evidence.locator: identification, textual tradition, production, physical description, contents, and editions
evidence.summary: Current specialist evidence for the shelfmark, Text D / Middle English Version A / Midland Version classification, current vol. 3 folios 54ra–63rb, production in England in 1350–1400, parchment support, three-volume extent, dimensions, absence of illustrations, adjacent works, and Whitelock edition. Its digitization field lists no manuscript facsimile.

evidence.citation: The Seven Sages of Rome Database, Midland Version.
evidence.locator: version description, composition, parent relationship, story sequence, and connected manuscript
evidence.summary: Specialist synthesis of Whitelock's findings: the sole surviving Midland witness is Dd.1.17; the version dates to 1350–1400; it belongs to Middle English Version A but is distinct from the Y Group; and its exact route may be through an unknown Middle English parent or directly from French prose A.

evidence.summary: Project-level evidence that the database is an AHRC-DFG scholarly resource synthesizing and updating the multilingual tradition, with named editors, cited sources, and an explicit work-in-progress warning.

open_questions.question: Can a complete color facsimile of all three current volumes of Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17 be obtained and retained locally?
next_steps: Ask Cambridge Special Collections whether complete existing photography, legacy microfilm, or an unindexed internal surrogate exists.

If ordering new photography, request all physical surfaces in all three volumes: bindings and spines where offered, boards or covers, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every manuscript leaf, and adjacent works—not only ff. 54ra–63rb.
Map CME00018 separately to vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb with exact image or canvas locators and first/end deep links.
Confirm retention and reuse terms in writing before caching repository-supplied files or publishing derivatives.
notes: No manuscript image was cached because no public complete-object deliverable or work-leaf image set was verified.

open_questions.question: Was the Midland Version adapted through a lost Middle English parent version, or directly from French prose Version A?
next_steps: Consult Whitelock's full textual analysis and subsequent stemmatic work before drawing a direct source-language lineage edge.
Retain Middle English Version A as the secure broader version family while leaving the immediate lost exemplar unidentified.

open_questions.question: How do the 1856 count of 424 leaves and continuous pages 1–848 map exactly onto the current three-volume extents of 261, 93, and 87 folios?
next_steps: Inspect current volume title leaves, foliation, collation, and rebinding history in a complete facsimile or onsite.
Consult modern codicological work on Dd.1.17 and verify whether the difference reflects inserted leaves, changed counting conventions, or later rearrangement.
Do not replace either cited extent or infer a page-by-page crosswalk without physical evidence.

open_questions.question: What initial keying method, correction history, and source-file terms apply to the U-M CME00018 encoding before the documented 2016 review?
next_steps: Ask the U-M Digital Collections team for item-level production metadata and any downloadable source package.
Compare the XML against Wright's complete pages and Whitelock's corrected edition without assuming OCR or manual keying.
Keep the 2016 TEMPHEAD date scoped to review rather than encoding creation.
